  • About|
  • Projects|
  • Portfolio

A personal relationship map that helps you remember what matters, stay intentional, and be a better friend.

starmap is a relationship management tool. Instead of treating your network as a flat contact list, it models your social world as a living graph: people, clusters, and connections.

The goal is simple: remember the little things, follow through on plans, and maintain relationships with more consistency and intention over time.

Curious? Go try starmap live at starmap.lol →

Timeline
March 2026–ongoing
Role
everything!
Recognition
40 users so far :)

MY USER JOURNEY

Why does it feel like I have no time?

Time tracking

It was the busiest summer of my life between my job, courses, and time with friends and family. I was tracking my time 24/7 with TogglTracker, and I was spending about 8% with friends - about 16% of my waking hours.

Being picky

It seemed like the answer was becoming choosier with who I chose to spend my time with. But how without data, how would I know who was real and fake?

FIRST SOLUTION

NOTION DOC

I wrote out the names of all 60+ "friends" I had along with journal entries on our friendship, how they made me feel, and things I should remember about them. I would update this whenever I saw them or something significant happened in our relationship.

PROBLEMS

It was such a long document and it wasn't well organized.

WHY STARMAP EXISTS

What if I made an app for that?

SECOND SOLUTION — your social world as a living graph

Focus Areas

three things that needed to work

01

Holding relationship context

Replacing the Notion doc with structured, per-person profiles.

02

Making data entry fast

The doc failed partly because updating it was a chore.

03

Visualizing the social graph

Seeing not just who you know, but how they connect.

Profiles & Nodes — Feature #1

making the notion doc obsolete

The first thing I built was the nodes feature — each person gets a profile card where you can log relationship notes, memories, and context. Visually, they exist as nodes on the map.

This accomplished what my Notion doc did, but in a clean, browsable, spatial format instead of an infinite scroll of text.

Import with AI — Feature #2

because nobody wants to type 60 names

The biggest friction point for new users was getting started — copying years of relationship notes into a new tool is a hard ask. I built a small Gemini wrapper that lets you paste your existing doc and have it auto-populate profiles.

One paste. Done.

What if I could visualize how people in my life know each other?

Constellations, Connections & Locations — Feature #3

what if you could see how your worlds overlap?

The graph view becomes most powerful when you can map relationships between people, not just people themselves.

  • Connections — shift-click two nodes to define how they know each other
  • Constellations — group people into communities (work, college, family, etc.)
  • Locations — track where people are as they move around

This turned Starmap from a glorified contact list into something that actually reflected how my social world was structured.

Design Showcase
Key Takeaways

Talk to your users directly, not just through analytics.

Emailing early users revealed that the features I'd spent the most time on weren't the ones people returned to. Direct conversations shaped the roadmap faster than any dashboard could.

Fit into existing workflows, don't replace them.

Most people who care about relationship context already live in Notion. A two-way sync isn't a nice-to-have — it's the difference between Starmap being a tool people adopt vs. one they try and abandon.

Interactions should feel spatial.

Shift-click to connect nodes. Draw on a canvas, don't fill out a form. The graph metaphor only works if the interactions reinforce it.

What's Next

Notion integration

Two-way sync so Starmap fits into existing workflows rather than asking people to build a new one from scratch.

Shift-click to connect

Replace the modal with a faster, more direct interaction — hold Shift, click two nodes, and they connect.

Structured user research

Targeted questions about which features people actually return to, and what's missing before they'd recommend it.

Try it live at starmap.lol →
